text: There are numerous stories of people surviving alone in the ocean for extended periods. One such story is that of Jose Salvador Alvarenga, a Mexican fisherman who spent over a year adrift in the Pacific Ocean before being rescued. Another story is that of Ron Ingraham, a Hawaiian fisherman who was stranded at sea for 12 days before being rescued. These stories highlight the incredible human spirit and the determination to survive against all odds. They also serve as a reminder of the dangers and unpredictability of life at sea.

There have been several instances of individuals surviving at sea for extended periods of time, often with limited resources and without the aid of technology. These stories are often referred to as "miracles" and are testaments to the resilience and determination of the human spirit. Here are a few recent examples:

* Louis Jordan, a 35-year-old sailor from South Carolina, was stranded at sea for five days after his boat capsized during a voyage in the Gulf Stream. He survived by rationing water, trapping and scooping up fish with his laundry, and rigging a makeshift mast and sail. He was eventually spotted by crew members on a German-flagged container ship and reunited with his father.
* Jose Salvador Alvarenga, a Mexican fisherman, was lost at sea for over a year after his boat drifted off course during a trip from Mexico's Pacific coast. He survived by drinking rainwater and eating raw birds, as well as using his fishing net to catch sea turtles. He was found on a remote atoll in the Marshall Islands by a passing boat and told CNN that his faith in God helped him survive.
* Ron Ingraham, a 67-year-old fisherman from Hawaii, was stranded at sea for 12 days after his boat was damaged in bad weather. He used a rope to pull himself back on board after a huge wave struck his vessel, but was unable to contact authorities. He was eventually picked up by a Coast Guard vessel and returned to shore weak, hungry, and dehydrated.
* Adrian Vasquez, an 18-year-old Panamanian boy, was stranded at sea for 26 days after his boat's engine died during a fishing trip. He survived by eating raw fish and drinking rainwater, and was eventually spotted by fishermen north of the Galapagos Islands.

text: There have been several instances of people surviving for extended periods of time at sea, often against incredible odds. In one case, a man named Louis Jordan was sailing alone when his boat capsized and he was left stranded in the middle of the ocean. He managed to survive for 66 days by using a makeshift mast and sail to catch fish and collect rainwater. Another man, Jose Salvador Alvarenga, was stranded at sea for 438 days after his fishing boat was blown off course and he was unable to communicate with anyone. He survived by eating raw birds, drinking rainwater, and eventually making his way to a remote island. Similarly, Ron Ingraham was stranded at sea for 12 days during a fishing trip, and Adrian Vasquez survived for 26 days at sea after his boat's engine died and he was left drifting in the Pacific Ocean. Finally, Hiromitsu Shinkawa was able to survive a tsunami that hit Japan in 2011 by clinging to the roof of his home and was eventually rescued after being spotted waving a red flag while floating on a pile of debris. These incredible stories of survival against the odds are a testament to the human spirit and the will to live in the face of adversity.

Police in Malegaon, India, have asked residents to photograph their cattle and provide their unique features, such as age and length of tail, to enforce a recent state-wide ban on the sale and consumption of beef. Over 100 owners have complied so far, with more lining up at police stations. "We are creating a database," said Mahesh Sawai, Deputy Superintendent of Malegaon Police. "If we get an information of a cow slaughter, we can quickly go to the resident's place and check whether it is there or not". This ruling followed a recent case of cow slaughter in Malegaon.

Police in the Indian city of Malegaon, Maharashtra, are requiring residents to submit 'mugshot' photos of their cattle and provide information about their unique features, following a state-wide ban on beef. The database is aimed at solving cow slaughter cases and enforcing the law. Over 100 owners have complied with the order so far. The ban includes the killing of bulls and bullocks, with a five-year prison sentence for the consumption or sale of beef. Beef traders protested the decision with a month-long strike, and now plan to file a case in the state's high court. Other Indian states are also tightening laws on cow slaughter.

Police in the Indian city of Malegaon are requiring identity cards for cows due to a state-wide ban on the sale and consumption of beef. Residents must provide a mugshot and information about their cow's unique features. The ruling came in the wake of a recent case of cow slaughter and has been praised by some as an effective way to enforce the law. However, beef traders and red meat lovers are not happy with the decision and have filed a case in the state's high court. Other Indian states have also tightened their laws on cow slaughter, with Haryana implementing a maximum punishment of 10 years in prison.
